summary: Shapiro shares a practical woodworking tip from building his own dining room table. When his pipe clamps were 4 inches too short, he realized that since pipe clamps are literally made of pipe, he could extend them using standard pipe couplers. He walks through the technique, noting the tradeoff of a bump at the coupler joint that can cause bowing, and offers workarounds like alternating clamp sides or using multiple short segments to distribute the bumps evenly.
key_points: Pipe clamps can be extended using standard pipe couplers since they're made of ordinary pipe
The coupler creates a bump that can cause bowing during glue-ups, so clamps should be placed on alternating sides
Using multiple short pipe segments with frequent couplers creates an even secondary surface instead of one problematic bump
Pipes can also be cut down and re-threaded at hardware stores for more versatile shorter lengths
money_quotes: I was gluing up our dining room table and hit a snag–the longest pipe clamps I have were 4" too short.
Well, fortunately I remembered that since they're made of PIPES, I can do all the usual pipe-y stuff to them, including put couplers on them.
In any case, grab some couplers that fit your pipe clamp pipes. You'll thank me for it.
key_insight: Pipe clamps are just pipes, so standard plumbing fittings like couplers can extend them when they're too short for a project.
